entries.title: *Alchymia* — page-unavailable placeholder (Plate 0)
entries.creator: Andreas Libavius
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Google Books digitisation (bub_gb_0WfRikJt9yQC) of Libavius's *Alchymia*]
entries.summary:
A **digitisation placeholder**, not an original leaf: the blue "page not available" card generated by the Google Books scan of Libavius's *Alchymia*, bearing only the legend *ALCHYMIA ANDREAE LIBAUII, RECOGNITA, EMENDATA, ET AUCTA, TUM DOGMATIBUS &…* and the names of author and printer.

## Iconography
A plain coloured field carrying the work's title and imprint in white type — a machine-generated stand-in where the corresponding page was not digitised. No historical image content whatsoever.

## Significance
Recorded honestly as a scanning artefact rather than as an apparatus plate. It is flagged here so that downstream users do not mistake the placeholder for one of Libavius's celebrated vessel or laboratory illustrations.

## For artists & game designers
No usable historical content — a digitisation stub only.

citations.text: Andreas Libavius, *Alchymia* (Frankfurt: Johann Saur for Peter Kopf, 1597; with the *Commentationum metallicarum* and *Singularia*).
Owen Hannaway, *The Chemists and the Word: The Didactic Origins of Chemistry* (Johns Hopkins, 1975) — on Libavius and the "ideal chemical house."
Bruce T. Moran, *Andreas Libavius and the Transformation of Alchemy* (2007).
Internet Archive

entries.title: *Alchymia* — text leaf (Latin) (Plate 25)
entries.creator: Andreas Libavius
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Google Books digitisation (bub_gb_0WfRikJt9yQC) of Libavius's *Alchymia*]
entries.summary:
A **letterpress text leaf** from Andreas Libavius's *Alchymia* (Frankfurt, 1597) — widely reckoned the first systematic textbook of chemistry. Dense Latin prose; no apparatus woodcut falls on this page.

## Iconography
Roman and italic type in the close, scholastic setting of the Frankfurt edition. No technical illustration is present on this leaf.

## Significance
Recorded honestly as a text page rather than as one of the work's famous apparatus plates — the every-twenty-fifth-page sampling fell on the discursive text, not the engraved vessels or the celebrated plan of the *domus chymica*. Libavius's achievement was precisely this: to set chymistry out as an ordered, teachable *ars*, defined, divided and described in plain didactic Latin.

## For artists & game designers
Usable as an authentic early-modern printed page or letterpress texture.

citations.text: Andreas Libavius, *Alchymia* (Frankfurt: Johann Saur for Peter Kopf, 1597; with the *Commentationum metallicarum* and *Singularia*).
Owen Hannaway, *The Chemists and the Word: The Didactic Origins of Chemistry* (Johns Hopkins, 1975) — on Libavius and the "ideal chemical house."
Bruce T. Moran, *Andreas Libavius and the Transformation of Alchemy* (2007).
Internet Archive

entries.title: *Alchymia* — Index (*Index I. partis*) (Plate 150)
entries.creator: Andreas Libavius
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Google Books digitisation (bub_gb_0WfRikJt9yQC) of Libavius's *Alchymia*]
entries.summary:
An **index leaf** — the *Index I. partis* — from Libavius's *Alchymia*: a dense two-column register of substances, operations and preparations with their folio references (here marked with later reader's highlights).

## Iconography
Letterpress in two narrow columns of headwords and page numbers, from *argentum philosophorum* through *aurum*, *cinabaris*, *mercurius* and on. No technical illustration is present.

## Significance
Recorded honestly as the work's index rather than as an apparatus plate. The index is itself a monument of Libavius's ordering instinct — the whole material world of the chymist alphabetised and made retrievable, the textbook's apparatus of reference.

## For artists & game designers
Usable as an authentic early-modern index or reference-page asset.

citations.text: Andreas Libavius, *Alchymia* (Frankfurt: Johann Saur for Peter Kopf, 1597; with the *Commentationum metallicarum* and *Singularia*).
Owen Hannaway, *The Chemists and the Word: The Didactic Origins of Chemistry* (Johns Hopkins, 1975) — on Libavius and the "ideal chemical house."
Bruce T. Moran, *Andreas Libavius and the Transformation of Alchemy* (2007).
Internet Archive

Bruce T. Moran, *Andreas Libavius and the Transformation of Alchemy* (2007).
Internet Archive

entries.title: *Alchymia* — page-unavailable placeholder (Plate 500)
entries.creator: Andreas Libavius
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Google Books digitisation (bub_gb_0WfRikJt9yQC) of Libavius's *Alchymia*]
entries.summary:
A **digitisation placeholder**, not an original leaf: the blue "page not available" card generated by the Google Books scan of Libavius's *Alchymia*, bearing only the legend *ALCHYMIA ANDREAE LIBAUII, RECOGNITA, EMENDATA, ET AUCTA, TUM DOGMATIBUS &…* and the names of author and printer.

## Iconography
A plain coloured field carrying the work's title and imprint in white type — a machine-generated stand-in where the corresponding page was not digitised. No historical image content whatsoever.

## Significance
Recorded honestly as a scanning artefact rather than as an apparatus plate. It is flagged here so that downstream users do not mistake the placeholder for one of Libavius's celebrated vessel or laboratory illustrations.

## For artists & game designers
No usable historical content — a digitisation stub only.
